EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures were above normal and precipitation was below normal across much of Utah during the month of September. As a result, drought conditions persisted across the entirety of eastern Utah and worsened to the exceptional category in some areas by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The extreme (D3) drought expanded slightly further north to encompass the majority of the northern tier of the area by the end of September. Elsewhere, the extreme drought persisted across all but the far southeast portion of the zone which worsened to the exceptional (D4) category by the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abnormally strong and cold low pressure system and associated cold front dropped south across the Northern Rockies and into the Four Corners. The low remained quasi-stationary for about a day before lifting northeast into the Plains. This system produced widespread showers of rain in the lower elevations and snow in the mountains, though the cold front dropped snow levels to approximately 7000 feet. Additionally, high winds led to not only reduced visibility in blowing snow, but also numerous downed trees and power outages in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 5 to 10 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts up to 12 inches estimated at the Kings Cabin SNOTEL site. In addition to the snowfall, winds gusted up to 68 MPH at times. The Emergency Manager in Manila measured 8 inches of snow with numerous power outages reported as a result of the strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure which originated off the coast of Baja, California moved inland and lifted across the Four Corners region. This storm led to moderate, wet snow across the higher elevations of the Eastern Uinta Mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and unseasonably moist low pressure system lifted across the Four Corners region. Favorable southerly flow during the onset of the event produced moderate to heavy snow over the southern mountains. Once cooler air moved into the region during the latter half of the storm, snowfall began to favor the northern and central Utah mountains. Wind gusts exceeded 45 MPH at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 10 to 20 inches with gusty winds up to 50 mph at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front progressed across eastern Utah and generally produced light snow across the region. However, instability associated with the front led to the development of a strong convective snow band which extended from the La Sal Mountains in southeast Utah to the West Elk Mountains in west-central Colorado. This band produced localized snowfall totals of up to 9 inches across the La Sal Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 8 to 9 inches of snow fell across the La Sal Mountains, much of that within 12 hours, with 2 to 4 inches of snow across the Abajos. Winds gusted up to 46 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm system brought heavy wet snow and very strong winds to the Northern Utah Mountains. Little Cottonwood Canyon was particularly hard hit, with the heavy snow and wind combination resulting in a major avalanche event that closed area ski resorts for two consecutive days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several SNOTEL sites received significant precipitation.  The Tony Grove Lake SNOTEL received 4.8 inches of liquid equivalent precipitation, or approximately 30 inches of snow, while the Lookout Peak SNOTEL received 4.4 inches of liquid equivalent precipitation, or approximately 28 inches of snow.  The Farmington Peak SNOTEL received 3.8 inches of liquid equivalent precipitation, or approximately 18 inches of snow. In addition, Snowbasin Resort reported 16 inches of snow. The Peter Sinks Rim weather station recorded a peak wind gust of 73 mph, while the Ogden Peak sensor recorded a peak wind gust of 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably moist Pacific shortwave trough lifted across eastern Utah and produced scattered to numerous showers beginning the night of May 10th. A break in the clouds the morning of May 11th fueled the atmosphere's instability and allowed showers and thunderstorms to redevelop across the region. The stronger storms produced frequent lightning and gusty winds to 58 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The automated weather station at the Canyonlands Airport in Moab, Utah recorded a peak wind gust of 58 MPH from a severe thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm system brought heavy wet snow and very strong winds to the Northern Utah Mountains. Little Cottonwood Canyon was particularly hard hit, with the heavy snow and wind combination resulting in a major avalanche event that closed area ski resorts for two consecutive days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations include 41 inches (6.79 inches of snow water equivalent) at Alta UDOT, 33 inches (5.52 inches of snow water equivalent) at Alta Collins, 29 inches (3.92 inches of snow water equivalent) at Snowbird, 18 inches (3.45 inches of snow water equivalent) at Big Cottonwood Spruces and 18 inches (2.68 inches of snow water equivalent) at Brighton Crest. In addition, the Hidden Peak station at Snowbird reported a peak wind gust of 100 mph, the Mount Baldy Avalanche Station reported a peak wind gust of 81 mph. The combination of heavy wet snow and persistent strong winds resulted in numerous avalanches across state Route 210 (Little Cottonwood Canyon Road). The Town of Alta remained under interlodge for 52 consecutive hours (the all-time consecutive hour record for the town), and Alta Ski Area and Snowbird remained closed for two consecutive days.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abnormal weather pattern for September developed, triggering a significant downslope wind event. Based on climatology and previous case study findings, this type of event most commonly occurs during the winter months. Downslope wind events are often confined to areas near canyon mouths, and usually impact only isolated areas with extreme winds. This event broke the mold in terms of both the early fall timing and extensive spatial coverage of the damage. High winds were reported as far south as the Tooele Valley, with high wind reports all along the Interstate 15 corridor stretching north into the Cache Valley. With the trees having their full canopy, the tree damage all along the Wasatch Front was extreme, with an estimated 4,500 trees damaged in Salt Lake County alone. Current estimates put the cost at $8.9 million. The storm led to 1 fatality, 20 direct injuries, and 4 indirect injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 68 mph was recorded at the Logan-Cache Airport. Widespread power outages were reported, with Rocky Mountain Power reporting 13,000 customers lost power. Numerous large trees were downed, including a tree estimated at 140 years old.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm complex moved across Washington County, including the City of St. George, during the evening hours, producing heavy rainfall and flash flooding, and an isolated severe wind gust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the late evening and early nighttime hours, intense rainfall was observed in St. George. The St. George, UT Coop observer reported 2.00 inches of rainfall in two hours, corresponding to a recurrence interval of approximately 200 years. The Santa Clara River at St. George rose from 3 cfs to 1,586 cfs in 3 hours. Interstate 15 was closed in both directions, with five to six feet of water reported across roadways in the area producing additional road closures, including the Red Hills Parkway. Many cars were stalled and stranded, several sink holes were reported, and power outages occurred.  St. George Rehabilitation was partially evacuated. Flood damage was reported in apartment complexes and basements. Monetary damage to public infrastructure was $3,082,501, with an additional $2,302,000 in damage to private property.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abnormal weather pattern for September developed, triggering a significant downslope wind event. Based on climatology and previous case study findings, this type of event most commonly occurs during the winter months. Downslope wind events are often confined to areas near canyon mouths, and usually impact only isolated areas with extreme winds. This event broke the mold in terms of both the early fall timing and extensive spatial coverage of the damage. High winds were reported as far south as the Tooele Valley, with high wind reports all along the Interstate 15 corridor stretching north into the Cache Valley. With the trees having their full canopy, the tree damage all along the Wasatch Front was extreme, with an estimated 4,500 trees damaged in Salt Lake County alone. Current estimates put the cost at $8.9 million. The storm led to 1 fatality, 20 direct injuries, and 4 indirect injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A historic downslope wind event impacted the Salt Lake and Tooele Valleys, with the most extensive damage observed across the Salt Lake Valley. A peak wind gust of 89 mph was recorded at The University of Utah Browning Building. Other notable peak wind gusts recorded included 77 mph at Salt Lake City International Airport, 68 mph at the Great Salt Lake Marina, 68 mph in the Salt Lake City Avenues, 67 mph by the Baccus Highway sensor, 67 mph by the I-215 at Olympus Cove sensor, and 59 mph by the Foothill Dr at Mario Capecchi Dr. sensor.  Widespread wind damage, downed trees, and power outages were reported. The Salt Lake Forester estimated more than 1500 trees fell on public land alone, not including private property, during the event.  Many of the trees were 60 to 100 years old. Liberty Park lost 69 trees, and the City Cemetery lost 255 trees. Over 180,000 power outages were reported along the Wasatch Front, with some locations taking days to around a week to have power restored due to the extensive damage. Salt Lake City schools were closed Tuesday through Thursday. The Capitol Complex was closed due to extensive tree damage, as were numerous parks, golf courses and recreation areas. Many COVID-19 testing sites were damaged and had to close, including all University of Utah testing sites. The only know fatality for the event occurred in South Salt Lake, when a 61-year-old truck driver unloading his truck was blown over by the wind, striking his head. In addition, Intermountain Healthcare  reported that 12 patients were treated for wind-related injuries. Damage and cleanup costs for the event tallied $4.3 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abnormal weather pattern for September developed, triggering a significant downslope wind event. Based on climatology and previous case study findings, this type of event most commonly occurs during the winter months. Downslope wind events are often confined to areas near canyon mouths, and usually impact only isolated areas with extreme winds. This event broke the mold in terms of both the early fall timing and extensive spatial coverage of the damage. High winds were reported as far south as the Tooele Valley, with high wind reports all along the Interstate 15 corridor stretching north into the Cache Valley. With the trees having their full canopy, the tree damage all along the Wasatch Front was extreme, with an estimated 4,500 trees damaged in Salt Lake County alone. Current estimates put the cost at $8.9 million. The storm led to 1 fatality, 20 direct injuries, and 4 indirect injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A historic downslope wind event unfolded along the Northern Wasatch Front. The peak wind gust for the event, not only in this zone, but across the state, was a 99 mph peak wind gust recorded by the US-89 at Park Lane sensor.   Other notable peak wind gusts included 97 mph at Farmington, 84 mph at Hill Air Force Base, 82 mph in North Brigham City, 75 mph at Brigham City Regional Airport, 75 mph at Centerville, and 64 mph at Ogden-Hinckley Airport. Extensive wind damage was reported along the entire Wasatch Front, with widespread downed trees, damaged roofs and power outages. Transportation was heavily impacted with all major north-south (Interstate 15, Interstate 84, and U.S. Highway 89) routes closed or restricted at times through the event.  At least 50 semitrailers were overturned, with four truck drivers with injuries taken to  hospitals. Over 180,000 power outages were reported along the Wasatch Front, with some locations taking days to around a week to have power restored due to the extensive damage. All school districts were closed Tuesday, with some closures continuing into Wednesday. Two Ogden hospitals reported significant damaged, and an Intermountain Life Flight helicopter was severely damaged. At least 20 manufactured homes were heavily damaged in Box Elder County, requiring residents to be transferred to shelters.  Damage and cleanup costs for the event tallied $4.3 million.

Current estimates put the cost at $8.9 million. The storm led to 1 fatality, 20 direct injuries, and 4 indirect injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 97 mph was recorded by the Logan Peak sensor. Other notable peak wind gusts include, 78 mph at Snowbasin - Ogden Peak, 75 mph at Powder Mountain, 74 mph at Big Mountain Pass, 69 mph at SR-16 @ MP1, and 63 mph at SR-30 at MP 124 Laketown. In addition, widespread downed trees were reported at Powder Mountain, on private property along UT-39 north of Causey Reservoir, and at Snowbasin Resort.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abnormal weather pattern for September developed, triggering a significant downslope wind event. Based on climatology and previous case study findings, this type of event most commonly occurs during the winter months. Downslope wind events are often confined to areas near canyon mouths, and usually impact only isolated areas with extreme winds. This event broke the mold in terms of both the early fall timing and extensive spatial coverage of the damage. High winds were reported as far south as the Tooele Valley, with high wind reports all along the Interstate 15 corridor stretching north into the Cache Valley. With the trees having their full canopy, the tree damage all along the Wasatch Front was extreme, with an estimated 4,500 trees damaged in Salt Lake County alone. Current estimates put the cost at $8.9 million. The storm led to 1 fatality, 20 direct injuries, and 4 indirect injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread downed trees were reported along the entire Park City ridgeline from above Canyons Village to Guardsman Pass at Deer Valley. Downed trees continued over the top of Guardsman Pass at Blood Lake and into the upper portion of Big Cottonwood Canyon, where the Redman Campground was closed for a couple days due to removal of downed trees. The Mountain Trails Foundation, that maintains hiking and biking trails in the Park City area, reported a record number of blowdowns, with 80 plus trees reportedly cleared from trails in this area.  A peak wind gust of 65 was recorded by the Alta - MT Baldy sensor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front progressed into eastern Utah and brought moderate snow to the Eastern Uinta Mountains. A secondary piece of energy lifted over the area during the latter half of the event which brought additional moderate snowfall to the La Sal and Abajo Mountains. A tightened gradient out ahead of and with the arrival of the front produced wind gusts in excess of 60 MPH at times which led to hazardous travel conditions in blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 10 to 12 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds gusted up to 60 MPH at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike most likely ignited a wildfire 7 miles northeast of Blanding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Alkali Ridge Fire began in mid July, most likely due to lightning, 7 miles northeast of Blanding and grew to 120 acres before it was contained the next day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures were above normal and precipitation was below normal across much of Utah during the month of November. As a result, drought conditions persisted across the entirety of eastern Utah and worsened to the exceptional category in some areas by the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad Pacific trough transitioned to a closed low as it approached the Four Corners Region and then lifted northeast across eastern Utah. This transition led to more widespread precipitation with accumulating snow occurring in most locations, particularly under heavier snow bands. However, there were some breaks in precipitation as drier air worked into the area at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 10 inches of snow fell at the East Willow Creek SNOTEL site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong and dynamic cold front moved through eastern Utah and western Colorado. Rain quickly switched over to snow for almost the entire forecast area. The heaviest snowfall fell in the La Sal Mountains. Once the front passed, bitterly cold temperatures moved in breaking many high and low temperature records.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 9 to 11 inches of snow fell in the La Sal Mountains with 4 inches in the Abajos. 2 inches fell in and around Monticello.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clear skies and light winds led to freezing temperatures across portions of northeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight lows ranged from 26 to 32 degrees with a low temperature of 30 degrees recorded at the Vernal Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A bitterly cold airmass settled overhead in the wake of a strong cold front. Clearing skies behind the front led to freezing temperatures across eastern Utah. The remaining susceptible valleys saw their season-ending freeze with this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight lows ranged from 21 to 28 degrees with a low temperature of 21 degrees recorded at the Blanding Municipal Airport. This was a season-ending freeze event for Southeast Utah. Temperatures remained below freezing throughout much of the 26th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clearing skies and light winds in the wake of a dry cold front led to a season-ending freeze across the eastern Uinta Basin in northeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight lows ranged from 20 to 25 degrees with a low temperature of 23 degrees recorded at the Vernal Regional Airport. This was a season-ending freeze event for the eastern Uinta Basin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front passage brought strong, damaging winds to the Uinta Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 93 mph was recorded by the Chepeta sensor.  Also, the Dry Ridge sensor in place for the East Fork Fire recorded a peak wind gust of 68 mph. In addition, the Incident Meteorologist on the East Fork Fire relayed reports of trees blown over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A bitterly cold airmass settled overhead in the wake of a strong cold front. Clearing skies behind the front led to freezing temperatures across eastern Utah. The remaining susceptible valleys saw their season-ending freeze with this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight lows ranged from 15 to 25 degrees with a low temperature of 19 degrees recorded at the Canyonlands Field Airport in Moab. This was a season-ending freeze event for the Grand Flat and Arches. Temperatures remained below freezing throughout much of the 26th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A bitterly cold airmass settled overhead in the wake of a strong cold front. Clearing skies behind the front led to freezing temperatures across eastern Utah. The remaining susceptible valleys saw their season-ending freeze with this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight lows ranged from 13 to 25 degrees. This was a season-ending freeze event for Canyonlands/Natural Bridges. Temperatures remained below freezing throughout much of the 26th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist and strong Pacific storm approached the Great Basin on Friday, June 5th. Hot, dry and windy conditions preceded this moisture which led to critical fire weather conditions across a large portion of the region Friday afternoon. Thunderstorms formed on the nose of the arriving moisture late in the afternoon on the 5th across the Four Corners region. A strong, northward moving outflow boundary was produced from these initial storms and survived to the Wyoming border by late in the evening. The fast movement of the boundary enhanced the wind threat from thunderstorms forming along it which led to numerous reports of strong outflow winds and localized damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 74 mph was measured by the automated weather station at the Canyonlands Field Airport in Moab.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A negatively tilted trough moved through eastern Utah on the morning and afternoon of Saturday, June 6th, causing a widespread severe weather event that would become the first derecho to impact the Great Basin in well over a decade. Severe storms produced large hail up to golf ball size in southeast Utah. Wind gusts upwards of 60 to 80 mph were reported on average with some gusts topping 80 to 100 mph in some areas due to strong downburst winds. There were multiple reports of uprooted trees, downed power lines, and several houses/cabins and vehicles were damaged by falling trees and/or large hail. Very heavy rain also fell which led to flash flooding in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced large hail estimated to be the size of quarters on the Seep Ridge Wildfire.

There were multiple reports of uprooted trees, downed power lines, and several houses/cabins and vehicles were damaged by falling trees and/or large hail. Very heavy rain also fell which led to flash flooding in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced large hail estimated to be the size of half dollars west-northwest of Blanding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures were above normal and precipitation was below normal across much of Utah during the month of November. As a result, drought conditions persisted across the entirety of eastern Utah and worsened to the exceptional category in some areas by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The extreme (D3) drought worsened to the exceptional (D4) category across the entirety of the zone by the end of November.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures were above normal and precipitation was below normal across much of Utah during the month of November. As a result, drought conditions persisted across the entirety of eastern Utah and worsened to the exceptional category in some areas by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The extreme (D3) drought persisted across the far northern portion of the zone while the exceptional (D4) category continued to expand to include the remainder of the area by the end of November.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist and strong Pacific storm approached the Great Basin on Friday, June 5th. Hot, dry and windy conditions preceded this moisture which led to critical fire weather conditions across a large portion of the region Friday afternoon. Thunderstorms formed on the nose of the arriving moisture late in the afternoon on the 5th across the Four Corners region. A strong, northward moving outflow boundary was produced from these initial storms and survived to the Wyoming border by late in the evening. The fast movement of the boundary enhanced the wind threat from thunderstorms forming along it which led to numerous reports of strong outflow winds and localized damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A social media report estimated a peak wind gust of 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large ridge of high pressure remained situated near the Four Corners region for several days. This resulted in scorching heat and dry weather across eastern Utah. Precipitable water values were at a record low for mid July which translated to single digit humidity at the surface. Record breaking high temperatures occurred through the period with overnight lows only dropping into the low to mid 80s below 5000 feet in the Arches and Grand Flat area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High temperatures of 104 to 109 degrees were reported by various automated weather stations at elevations below 5000 feet on July 11th, 12th and 13th. Additionally, overnight lows ranged from 80 to 83 degrees on the morning of the 12th and 13th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moved through southern Utah bringing largely light to moderate snow accumulations, but an area of heavy snow fell across the northern Grand Staircase area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Coop observers in Escalante and Kodachrome Basin State Park reported 10 and 7.2 inches of snow, respectively, with most of that snow falling in about a 12 hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low level moisture trapped across eastern Utah, combined with clear skies and light winds, led to areas of dense fog in some of the northeast and east-central valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog was observed across the Eastern Uinta Basin including at the Vernal Regional Airport as visibility was reduced to less than one quarter of a mile for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low level moisture trapped across eastern Utah, combined with clear skies and light winds, led to areas of dense fog in some of the northeast and east-central valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog was observed across the Grant Flat and Arches region including at the Canyonlands Field Airport as visibility was reduced to less than one quarter of a mile for several hours.
